sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 37-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of flank pain radiating to the groin, which is intermittent and worsens with increased fluid intake and physical activity.
The pain is described as a 4 out of 10, but has been as high as a 2 out of 10.
He denies nausea, vomiting, fever, and chills.
He reports a history of passing kidney stones in the past, including one that required surgery.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of kidney stones and has had them himself in the past, including passing some on his own and undergoing surgery.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms mentioned.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking ibuprofen for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is elevated, but this is likely due to pain.
Physical exam: The patient has CVA tenderness on the right side, consistent with the location of the kidney stone.
Test Results: The patient had a CT scan that showed a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter with no evidence of high flow nephrosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of a kidney stone, measuring 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter.
Problem list: Kidney stone; active.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen as before for pain management, and I will also prescribe tramadol 50 mg as needed for breakthrough pain.
The patient should continue to use a strainer when urinating to try to catch the stone.
I will order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out infection.
I will see the patient in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment, including lithotripsy if necessary.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment.
